:::: {.panel-tabset .nav-pills} ## Prison Population
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

New Court Commits vs. Parole Revocations

While the public often envisions a prison population primarily made up of individuals serving long sentences for serious crimes, the reality is far more nuanced. In particular, the distinction between new court commits—those incarcerated for a new crime—and parole revocations—those returned to prison for violating the conditions of their parole—is a critical one. Understanding the proportion of these two groups within the prison population can provide valuable insights into the effectiveness of the state's criminal justice policies, including sentencing and parole practices.


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Prison Population Trends


``` ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

## Parole Eligibility
::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

How is Parole Eligibility Determined?

By law, a nonviolent offender convicted of a Class D felony with an aggregate sentence of one to five years is eligible for parole review after serving 15% or two months of their original sentence, whichever is longer. The time before the first parole review for all felonies committed after December 3, 1980, is determined based on the sentence being served. For sentences ranging from 1 year, up to but not including 2 years, the time before the first review is 4 months. For sentences ranging from 2 years up to and including 39 years, the time before the first review is 20% of the sentence. For sentences of 39+ years, including life sentences, the time before the first review is 8 years. In the case of a persistent felony offender with a class A, B, or C felony, the time before the first review is 10 years.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Parole-Eligible Prison Population

In 2020, there were 6,227 people who were eligible for parole but remained incarcerated for a new crime. This group made up 33% of the prison population.


``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

People who are Eligible for Parole

``` ::: ::: ::: {.panel-tabset} ### Race and Ethnicity ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, White, non-Hispanic people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 75% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Gender ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, male people made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 90% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ### Age ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

In 2020, people who were between the ages of 25-34 years old made up the largest proportion of those eligible for parole yet still incarcerated for a new criminal offense, comprising 34% of the parole-eligible population serving time for new crimes.

``` ::: ::: ::: ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Something like “After controlling for differences in ages, offenses, and sentence length, 66% of White people, 40% of Black people, and 55% of Hispanic people are released before/on parole eligibility date.
